text: The robotic side cart is positioned to the left of the patient at the foot of the bed. The surgical team utilizes a three-arm robotic technique, with an assistant using a five-millimeter port.
A twelve millimeter camera trocar is positioned centrally between two eight millimeter robotic trocars, establishing the visual field for the robotic hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon reduces the small bowel that is incarcerated in the perineal hernia, followed by taking down adhesions to further mobilize the bowel.
The surgeon fully visualizes the extent of the perineal hernia, revealing the anatomical boundaries and surrounding tissue structures.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter, which is marked with a lighted stent, and visualizes the bilateral iliac vessels during the robotic hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon initiates preperitoneal dissection on the right side of the pelvic wall, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the peritoneal layer from underlying structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection anteriorly towards the patient's left side, creating a peritoneal flap, with visible separation of the peritoneal tissue.

The surgeon drops the peritoneal flap over the hernia defect, obliterating the space and creating a suspensory membrane for the mesh. Interrupted absorbable sutures are used to secure the flap in place.
The surgeon measures the hernia space and introduces a coated polypropylene mesh, marked at the midline, into the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the sacral promontory and anchors the coated polypropylene mesh to it using a non-absorbable suture.
The surgeon fixates the mesh to the pelvic sidewall, taking care to avoid the ureter and iliac vessels during the procedure.
The surgeon fixates the mesh to Cooper's ligaments on both sides, ensuring secure attachment within the surgical field.
The surgeon fixates the mesh to the median umbilical ligament using an absorbable suture, completing the mesh placement in the hernia repair procedure.
